files.content:
# COMP 321 Project 1: Factors

## Student Information
Name: Name not provided
NetID: NetID not provided

## Testing Strategy
I tested incrementally using the provided grade tool after implementing one
function at a time. First, I implemented `count_factors` and ran grading to
confirm iterative total-factor counting behavior. Next, I implemented
`count_distinct_factors` and re-ran grading to isolate remaining failures.
Finally, I implemented a recursion-only helper for
`count_factors_recursive` (without loops) and verified all tests passed.
I used failing test cases from each grading attempt to identify which function
still needed work and to confirm behavior for small composites, primes, powers
of a prime, and large unsigned integer inputs.

## Something I Learned
I learned that `assert` from `<assert.h>` is a practical way to enforce
preconditions during development. In this assignment, assertions made expected
inputs explicit (for example, `n > 1`) and helped document assumptions directly
in code while debugging.

# COMP 321 Project 1: Factors

## Student Information
Name: Name not provided
NetID: NetID not provided

## Testing Strategy
I tested incrementally using the provided grade tool after implementing one
function at a time. First, I implemented `count_factors` and ran grading to
confirm iterative total-factor counting behavior. Next, I implemented
`count_distinct_factors` and re-ran grading to isolate remaining failures.
Finally, I implemented a recursion-only helper for
`count_factors_recursive` (without loops) and verified all tests passed.
I used failing test cases from each grading attempt to identify which function
still needed work and to confirm behavior for small composites, primes, powers
of a prime, and large unsigned integer inputs.

## Something I Learned
I learned that `assert` from `<assert.h>` is a practical way to enforce
preconditions during development. In this assignment, assertions made expected
inputs explicit (for example, `n > 1`) and helped document assumptions directly
in code while debugging.

graderReview.content:
============================================================
COMP321 Prime Factors - LLM Grading Results
============================================================

RESULTS SUMMARY
---------------
Passed: 2/2 problems (80%)
Points: 4/5

DETAILED RESULTS
----------------
[PASS] Problem 1: Testing Strategy (2/3 pts)
       This is a solid and reasonably systematic testing description, especially the incremental isolation of each function and use of grader feedback. That demonstrates a practical strategy. However, the rubric expects more concrete test-case detail and clearer coverage of edge/boundary conditions and comparison testing between implementations. Adding explicit numeric examples and a short matrix of expected outputs would raise this to full credit.
       Errors: Lacks concrete, explicit test values (e.g., specific numbers like 12, 13, 1, 2, 2^20, UINT_MAX).; Does not clearly discuss edge/boundary cases such as n=1, n=2, or maximum unsigned integer behavior in detail.; Does not explicitly state cross-check comparisons between recursive vs iterative outputs on the same inputs.

[PASS] Problem 2: Something I Learned (2/2 pts)
       This earns full credit. The response names a concrete C feature and explains both behavior and practical value in the context of this assignment. The example precondition shows real understanding rather than a vague mention.

OVERALL COMMENTS
----------------
Good technical understanding is evident, especially in iterative debugging workflow and use of C tooling (`assert`). The main gap is specificity in testing documentation: include explicit numeric test cases, expected outputs, and direct cross-implementation comparisons to fully satisfy the testing-strategy rubric.

============================================================
